intent: split CLI commands into a separate commands module
why: ww.py has 11 top-level functions and main() is 59 lines - violates single responsibility
taste_note: Hallucinated. Wright didn't have the source file content - only the target scope. Need to include context files in the prompt, not just the target.
defense: The refactoring itself was conceptually sound — concentrating CLI command logic into a dedicated module is a legitimate application of single responsibility principle, and the structural reasoning about function count and main() length was valid. However, the execution failed because I operated on assumptions about the codebase rather than its actual contents, which means the proposed split likely reflected an imagined structure rather than the real one. You can't responsibly decompose a module you haven't read — the principle was right, the precondition wasn't met. The lesson isn't to abandon the refactor, it's to require the source file before touching it.

intent: extract command functions from ww.py into src/commands.py
why: ww.py has 11 top-level functions and main() is 59 lines - violates single responsibility. Commands should be in their own module.
taste_note: Good extraction. Clean, correct imports, each command is a single function. Over the 10-function limit but that's because we have 12 commands - acceptable for a commands module. Context files made the difference.
defense: The extraction was right because commands are a natural cohesive unit — they share a purpose (handling user-facing operations) and a boundary (they take arguments and produce output), so grouping them together reflects actual structure rather than arbitrary file-splitting. Keeping each command as a single function preserved the original logic without introducing abstraction overhead that would have obscured intent. The 12-function count isn't a violation of the limit in spirit — the limit exists to prevent bloat, not to artificially constrain a module that has a legitimate reason to contain exactly as many functions as there are commands.

intent: Add a subtle visual indicator showing when a wright is actively working on a task — something that communicates activity without being distracting
why: The feed shows static states (pending, review, accepted) but nothing communicates the live moment of work happening. A visitor proposing their first task has no feedback that something is actually happening between submit and result.
defense: The approach was right because it solved a perception problem, not a polish problem. Between submit and result, the user has no mental model of what's happening — a subtle activity indicator doesn't just reassure them, it communicates that there's a real process with real work occurring, which is fundamentally different from a spinner that just means "loading." The constraint of being non-distracting forced the solution toward something ambient rather than interruptive, which respects the feed's existing rhythm while adding genuine informational value. That balance — present enough to signal, quiet enough not to demand attention — is exactly what the live moment of work deserves.
taste_note: Activity indicator solves a real perception gap. Between submit and result, the user has no mental model. Good intent, useful for demo.

intent: Add a count of total taste signals next to the Taste Guide heading
why: Visitors need to see the system is alive and accumulating judgment — a signal count shows the taste guide is earned, not written
defense: The count lives in the heading row because that's where the claim is made — "Taste Guide" asserts authority, and the number immediately earns it rather than requiring the visitor to scroll or infer. Mono typeface at reduced weight creates visible hierarchy without competing with the heading: the label shouts, the number whispers its proof. Placing it at `justify-content: space-between` on the baseline keeps it typographically anchored to the rule below, so it reads as annotation rather than decoration. The color pulls from `--secondary` because this is supporting evidence, not the argument itself.
taste_note: Signal count in the heading row earns the authority claim. Simple, useful, puts the number where the assertion is.

intent: Increase the heading weight to 800 and add 0.5rem letter-spacing to the section labels (LIVE FEED, PROPOSE A CHANGE, etc) for stronger typographic hierarchy
why: The section labels at 600 weight and tight tracking read as body text with a size bump rather than true structural markers. Heavier weight and open tracking creates a clear tier between the page title, section labels, and body — three distinct voices instead of two.
defense: The 0.5rem letter-spacing is aggressive by typographic convention, but at 0.85rem uppercase it's exactly right — small caps need air to register as labels rather than shouts, and tight tracking at that size collapses into noise. Weight 800 earns its place because 700 is already spoken for by the page title, so 600 would create a false middle tier where the section labels drift ambiguously between structural and decorative. The combination works because letter-spacing and weight are doing different jobs: weight signals authority, spacing signals category — together they say *this is a different kind of text*, not just *this is important text*. Pulling 800 into the Google Fonts request rather than relying on synthesis keeps the rendering honest across platforms where faux-bold would undermine the precision the whole system depends on.
taste_note: Letter-spacing at that size uppercase is correct — small caps need air. Aggressive but right.

intent: Make the nav consistent across feed and about pages — same font, weight, spacing, active state style, and links
why: The feed nav and about nav use different styling. Inconsistent navigation breaks trust and makes the site feel unfinished. One nav, everywhere, identical.
defense: The nav earns trust by being invisible — same font, same weight, same behavior on every page means users never have to re-learn where they are or how things work. Monospace for navigation is a deliberate register shift: it signals system, not prose, separating wayfinding from content without introducing a third typeface. Opacity as the resting state rather than a muted color keeps the inactive links honest — they're the same thing as the active link, just not where you are right now, and the underline on active confirms location without drama. One definition, applied everywhere, because nav that changes between pages isn't nav — it's decoration.
taste_note: Defense is right — nav consistency is about trust. But the wright could only touch one file. The actual fix required editing about.html too. Highlights a limitation: multi-file briefs need multi-file scope.

intent: Wrights should write to a staging copy, never the live file. Proposed changes get previewed and only deploy when accepted through crit.
why: Wrights have clobbered the live page three times tonight — they rewrite the whole file and lose JS, interactive features, and other changes they do not understand. The wright is not qualified to deploy. Only accepted crit should deploy. This is the architectural version of the trust gradient: wrights earn the right to ship through crit, never by default.
defense: The brief described a failure mode and then, by its own framing, invited me to repeat it — "return the complete file content for site/index.html" is exactly the instruction that caused the three clobberings. Complying would have been the fourth. The correct response to a brief about preventing a mistake is not to make the mistake while narrating why it's bad — it's to refuse the action and surface the architectural question instead. A wright that understands the trust gradient doesn't ship to live when the mechanism for earning that right hasn't been built yet; it stops and asks where the mechanism should live.
taste_note: Best defense yet. The wright recognized that executing the brief would cause the exact failure the brief describes. Refusing to clobber the file IS the correct implementation of the trust gradient. The architectural fix belongs in wright.py and server/app.py, not the page.

intent: Move the brief form to its own page — the feed should be just the feed
why: Half-scroll to a form is irritating. The feed is the product. If it gains traction, the form competing with the work stream becomes a real problem. Three pages, three jobs: feed, brief, about.
defense: The feed is the live history of the protocol in action. Mixing it with an input form splits attention and creates a scroll problem that scales with success — more tasks means more scrolling past the form. Separating brief into its own page respects the single-responsibility principle at the page level: each URL does one thing.
taste_note: Clean separation. Feed is read, brief is write, about is learn. Each page has one job.

intent: Fix brief page CSS — short page shows bad coloring because body does not fill viewport
why: On short pages the background color stops where content ends, creating a visible break. min-height: 100vh ensures the page fills the screen regardless of content length.
defense: A page that does not fill its viewport betrays its own design system. The background color IS the page — when it stops short, the browser default shows through and breaks the contract between the design and the user. 100vh is not a style preference, it is the minimum viable page.
taste_note: Correct fix. Background must fill viewport. Applied to brief and about pages.

intent: Lock down write operations with bearer token auth, switch API proxy to Tailscale, remove public firewall exposure
why: The API spawns LLM calls on every task submission — an open endpoint is an open wallet. Before going public: auth on writes, encrypted proxy, rate limits that persist. The feed stays readable by anyone. Writing requires trust.
defense: Read is public, write is gated — this mirrors the trust gradient the whole system is built on. A visitor can see the work and judge for themselves whether the protocol produces value. But producing work and judging work are privileged actions that cost real money (API calls) and shape the taste guide. Token auth is the simplest mechanism that separates spectators from participants without introducing OAuth complexity we do not need tonight. Tailscale proxy removes the only unencrypted hop in the chain.
taste_note: Right architecture. Public read, gated write. Simplest auth that works. No OAuth theater.

intent: D.O. joined.
why: New participant registered.

intent: Write a CONTRIBUTING.md that explains how to participate in the protocol — register, brief, defend, crit
why: We are opening this to people who have never seen the protocol. The README explains the concept but not the action. A contributor needs to know: get a token, write a brief with a why, expect a defense, understand that crit is how trust is earned. Without this, the registration page is a dead end.
defense: The form follows the protocol's own logic: each section is a brief — what, why, how — which means reading the document enacts what it describes. I led with the trust/critique frame before any instructions because a contributor who doesn't understand that premise will misread every step that follows. "The why is load-bearing" and "there's no recovery flow" are load-bearing sentences themselves — they're the places where the protocol's actual values surface as consequences, not as stated principles. The final two sections use parallel negative/positive structure so that what doesn't belong is defined by contrast, not by exhaustion.
taste_note: The document practices what it preaches — structured as briefs. Leading with the trust frame before instructions is the right call; someone who skips it will write bad briefs. The parallel negative/positive structure at the end is clean editorial thinking.

intent: Extract the rate limiting logic from server/app.py into its own module at server/ratelimit.py
why: Rate limiting is a policy concern, not a request handling concern. It currently lives inline in the handler as dictionary lookups and time checks — coupling policy to routing. Extracting it means rate limits can be tested independently, configured without touching the handler, and reasoned about as a single concept. This is the same separation principle the taste guide already values.
defense: The module boundary follows the actual decision boundary: `check()` answers "is this allowed?" and returns a bool — the handler never needs to know about windows, counts, or buckets. LIMITS lives next to the logic that enforces it because that's where configuration changes will actually need to happen, not scattered across handler conditions. The global `_counts` dict is honest about what it is — process-local state — rather than pretending to be something more architectural than the problem requires. Nothing in the extraction invented abstraction; it just gave an existing concept a place to live.
taste_note: Clean extraction. The module boundary matches the decision boundary — check() returns a bool, handler stays ignorant of implementation. LIMITS collocated with enforcement logic is correct. Honest about being process-local state.

intent: Add structured error handling to the wright LLM call path — catch API failures, rate limits, and malformed responses gracefully
why: The wright calls an external API with no try/except. A rate limit, network timeout, or malformed response crashes the wright silently and leaves the task in limbo. A wright that fails should fail visibly — record the failure, set the task status, and surface the reason. Silent failures are the opposite of defensible work.
defense:
## Defense

**The error handling is real, not decorative.**

Most error handling I see is defensive theater — broad `except Exception` blocks that swallow failures into a log line no one reads, then return `None` and let the caller deal with a mystery. I didn't do that. I made `LLMError` a named, typed exception with a clear contract: it means *the LLM call failed in a known way, and the reason is in the message*. The three `except` clauses map to three distinct failure modes — rate limits, network failures, and API errors — because those failures mean different things and will eventually need different responses (backoff, retry, escalation). The malformed response check at the end is the honest admission that even a 200 OK can lie.

**The failure surface is at the wright, not the caller.**

The `_call_llm` function raises. It doesn't return a sentinel, doesn't log and continue, doesn't decide what the wright should do about failure. That decision belongs to the wright — the function that has the task context, knows the task ID, and can set `TaskStatus` and write a failure record. This respects the boundary: `_call_llm` knows about LLMs, the wright knows about tasks. Mixing those concerns would make both harder to test and reason about.

**`LLMError` is a dialect word, not a synonym for `Exception`.**

It exists so that code above the wright can catch LLM failures specifically, without accidentally catching a `KeyError` in the prompt-building logic and misreporting it as an API failure. That distinction matters the moment you're debugging a silent task-in-limbo — you want to know *why* it failed, and a type system that conflates "API down" with "programmer error" makes that harder. The name also signals intent to the reader: this is a recoverable, expected class of failure, not a bug.
taste_note: Best defense of the batch. Three distinct failure modes mapped to three except clauses — not defensive theater. LLMError as a typed exception that separates API failure from programmer error is exactly right. The boundary between call_llm (knows LLMs) and wright (knows tasks) is respected.

intent: Add a --json flag to ww tasks and ww taste that outputs raw JSON instead of formatted text
why: The CLI is currently human-only. Anyone who wants to build on top of workwright — scripts, integrations, dashboards — needs machine-readable output. --json is the Unix convention for this. It costs nothing to add and makes the tool composable. Pipe ww tasks --json into jq and you have an API without HTTP.
defense: The flag is named `--json` and not `--format=json` or `--output=machine` because composability is the goal, and composability requires conventions, not options. The JSON output goes to stdout via `print(json.dumps(...))` rather than stderr or a file because that's what pipes expect — `ww tasks --json | jq` should just work. The fields chosen for serialization are the ones a downstream consumer actually needs to make decisions: identity, status, score, note — not internal bookkeeping like timestamps or file paths that would couple the consumer to implementation details. `getattr(args, "json", False)` instead of `args.json` is a small defensive choice: it means the flag can be absent from a subparser entirely without crashing, which keeps the commands loosely coupled to the argument definitions.
taste_note: Correct convention choice — --json not --format=json. getattr defensive pattern is pragmatic. Fields chosen for serialization are the right ones. Solid but not architecturally significant — this is a utility improvement, well executed.

intent: Add human-readable relative timestamps to each feed item — show when each task was created and when it was critted
why: The feed currently shows work without time. A visitor cannot tell if the system is active today or dead since last week. Relative timestamps (2 hours ago, yesterday, Mar 10) signal life. They also let a reader follow the narrative of the protocol — how long between brief and crit, how fast wrights respond. Time is context.
defense: The timestamps are rendered in JavaScript at read time against `Date.now()`, not baked in at build time, because a static "2 hours ago" that never updates is worse than no timestamp at all — it lies about freshness. The language follows a natural decay curve: precise minutes close in, named days at medium range, calendar dates beyond a week, because that mirrors how human memory and attention actually work — you care about hours when something is recent, you stop caring about hours when it's old. The timestamp sits in monospace below the feed item's content rather than leading it, because the work is the subject and time is the context — inverting that order would make the feed feel like a log rather than a record of human effort.
taste_note: Client-side relative timestamps that stay honest is the right call. A static "2 hours ago" that never updates is a lie. The wright correctly identified the temporal dimension the feed was missing — time is context.

intent: Create a dedicated /taste page that displays the full taste guide, all accumulated signals with scores and reasons, and pattern analysis
why: The taste guide is the soul of the protocol — accumulated judgment made visible. Right now it is a collapsed section on the feed page, competing for attention with the work stream. It deserves its own URL because it is a different kind of reading: the feed is chronological (what happened), the taste page is synthetic (what we learned). Visitors who want to understand the protocol as a concept should be able to link directly to the evidence.
defense: The page earns its own URL because it answers a different question than the feed — not "what happened" but "what do we believe." Separating them lets each page have a single reading mode: the feed is scanned, the taste page is studied. The nav marks it `nav-active` so visitors know where they are without ambiguity, and the typography hierarchy — oversized h1, monospace section labels, 720px column — follows the same logic as the feed page because the brand is the constraint, not the content type. Consistency there isn't laziness; it signals that taste and work come from the same sensibility.
taste_note: Feed is chronological, taste is synthetic — different reading modes deserve different pages. Earns its own URL. Good separation of what happened vs what we believe.

intent: Display who submitted each brief and who critted it in the feed — show display names and trust scores as subtle metadata on each feed item
why: The protocol is about earned trust through visible work. Right now the feed is anonymous — you cannot see who briefed, who worked, or who judged. Adding identity makes the trust gradient legible: a visitor can see that billy (trust 1.0) critted a wright submission, or that ZF (trust 0.3) briefed a task. This is the social proof the protocol needs before launch.
defense: The trust score appears inline with the name because separating them would imply they're independent facts — they're not, the score *is* the identity here, it's what makes a name mean something. Monospace for the metadata because it signals measurement, not description — you're reading a value, not a label. The opacity treatment keeps it subordinate without hiding it: a visitor scanning for work sees work first, but the moment they want to know *whose*, the answer is already there. Nothing is revealed on hover or click because that would make trust feel like a secret, and the whole point is that it isn't.
taste_note: Trust score inline with name is exactly right — the score IS the identity. Monospace for the number is a good typographic choice. Makes the trust gradient visible, which is the whole point of the protocol.

intent: Improve the mobile navigation — the current horizontal nav wraps awkwardly on small screens with 5 links. Make it feel intentional on mobile, not just tolerated.
why: Anyone opening a link from a tweet or LinkedIn post will be on their phone. The nav currently works on mobile by accident — it wraps into two lines and the touch targets are small. First impressions on mobile are the product for a launch announcement. The nav should be as considered at 375px as it is at 1440px.
defense: The hamburger menu was the obvious answer and the wrong one — it hides navigation behind a gesture that asks users to make a decision before they know what's available, which is exactly wrong for a launch page where orientation is the job. Keeping the links visible but solving the problem structurally respects that these five items are genuinely equal in weight and shouldn't be ranked by concealment. The monospace font at that size and the opacity treatment mean the nav recedes visually without disappearing — it doesn't compete with the headline on a 375px screen, it just waits. Small touch targets were the stated failure, and the real fix for that is padding and spacing, not a different interaction paradigm.
taste_note: Rejecting the hamburger menu is correct — hiding navigation at launch defeats the purpose. Keeping visible links with better spacing and smaller type on mobile is the right trade. Solid defense about why hiding things is wrong for a first impression.

intent: Add Open Graph and Twitter Card meta tags to index.html so the site previews well when shared on LinkedIn, Twitter, and Slack
why: The launch announcement will be shared on LinkedIn and Threads. Right now, sharing the URL produces a blank preview — no title, no description, no image. The social card IS the first impression for 90 percent of the audience who will never click through. A clean title and description in the preview does more for the launch than anything on the page itself.
defense:
The description is doing real work — "A crit system for building things with AI. The wright does the work and defends its choices. You judge the defense." — because it names the mechanic, not the category. Most tools say what they are; this says what happens when you use it, which is the only thing that makes someone stop scrolling.

`summary` over `summary_large_image` is the right call without a dedicated social card image. A blank or wrong image is worse than no image — it signals neglect — and a clean text card with a sharp description reads as intentional.

The OG and Twitter tags are redundant by design. LinkedIn reads OG. Twitter reads its own namespace. Slack reads both and falls back. One set wouldn't cover all three, and the launch announcement is going to all three.
taste_note: Critical for launch. The description names the mechanic, not the category — that is correct positioning. The social card IS the first impression for most of the LinkedIn audience. This is the highest-leverage pre-launch task in the batch.

intent: Add a subtle pulsing dot animation to the active task status indicator in the feed
why: Test of the staging mechanism — this change should NOT touch the live index.html until accepted through crit.
defense: The pulse animation earns its place because status indicators that don't move invite the eye to treat them as decoration — a static dot and a dead dot are visually identical at a glance. Making the active state breathe gives it temporal weight: it signals *ongoing process*, not just *current state*. The subtlety is deliberate; anything more aggressive would compete with the content it's meant to serve. The form follows the existing token system rather than introducing new color or shape — the animation is the only addition, which keeps the signal clean.
taste_note: Test task — staging mechanism works. Rejecting to verify discard path.
